rationale: Goldfarb and Tucker (2011) is cited in the core framework section to motivate the paper's selection-effect mechanism, not just in a background list. The citing paper explicitly links its own channel-substitution tests to that earlier finding that offline ad bans shifted demand online. This prior result is used to frame hypotheses and interpret post-shutdown pathway shifts, giving the citation a meaningful conceptual role.
relation_description: The citing paper uses Goldfarb and Tucker (2011) as prior evidence that banning one advertising channel can induce substitution into another channel. It extends that substitution logic to a different policy shock, the FTC shutdown of fake news ads, by testing whether consumers shift to regular ads and organic pathways.
